5 min read
Just now

--

Not the models. Not the prompts. The layer underneath — and the GitHub data shows exactly when it happened.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Python designs the agent. Rust increasingly runs it. 🦀For two years, “Rust for AI” meant a handful of enthusiasts hand-rolling tensor kernels for fun. Python owned everything: training, fine-tuning, orchestration, the tutorials, the frameworks, the mindshare. If you said “I’m building my agent in Rust” in early 2024, that was a personality trait, not an architecture decision.
That changed in the first quarter of 2026, and it changed fast enough to show up in the star-velocity data on GitHub’s fastest-growing AI repos. Seven significant Rust agent projects launched in under sixty days. The 2023–2024 wave of Rust AI tools averaged around 25 stars a day. The 2026 wave averages roughly 400 — a 16x jump, and it holds even if you strip out the corporate-backed repos from Nvidia and Google.
Something shifted. It wasn’t “Rust is faster,” because Rust has always been faster. It’s what moved to Rust that matters.
The Stack Didn’t Flip. It Split. Here’s the pattern once you look past the headlines: nobody serious is rewriting model training or fine-tuning in Rust. That’s Python’s home, and it’s staying Python’s home for the foreseeable future — the ecosystem lead is a decade deep…
